title: 9 International ETFs for Investors Looking Beyond U.S. Stocks
author: Selena Maranjian
description: The article recommends nine international ETFs for portfolio diversification beyond U.S. stocks, citing geopolitical risks and tariff disruptions. It highlights ETFs offering dividend income, broad market exposure, and emerging market opportunities, with particular emphasis on Vanguard and iShares funds as accessible alternatives to traditional S&P 500 index funds.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Recommended as a broad international option excluding U.S. stocks with 8,650+ holdings, offering strong diversification with low fees.
Offers comprehensive global exposure but noted as having limited diversification benefit due to heavy U.S. stock concentration (9 of top 10 holdings).
Highlighted as particularly attractive for offering meaningful dividend yield combined with strong growth track record.
Recommended as a solid choice for developed markets exposure with 2,600+ stocks across 21 developed markets and low fees.
Recommended for emerging market exposure with small-cap growth potential, though noted as more volatile.
Presented as a dividend-focused international option with a strong track record.
Recommended for emerging market exposure with low fees.
Mentioned as a dividend-focused option but noted as having limited track record since launching in 2021.
Recommended as a low-fee option for emerging market exposure.

title: Emerging Market Showdown: IEMG Offers Lower Fees Compared to EEM
author: Jake Lerch
description: IEMG and EEM are both emerging market ETFs with nearly identical sector exposure, but IEMG offers a significant cost advantage with a 0.09% expense ratio versus EEM's 0.72%, plus a higher dividend yield of 2.6% versus 2.1%. IEMG holds over twice as many stocks including small-caps, while EEM focuses on large- and mid-cap companies. Though EEM slightly outperformed over one year (26.2% vs 25.5%), IEMG's lower fees and broader diversification make it more attractive for most cost-conscious investors.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: IEMG is presented as the superior choice for most investors due to its significantly lower expense ratio (0.09% vs 0.72%), higher dividend yield (2.6% vs 2.1%), broader portfolio with 2,725 holdings including small-caps, and comparable five-year risk-adjusted returns despite lower one-year performance.

EEM is presented as a viable but less attractive alternative, with its main advantage being slightly better one-year performance (26.2% vs 25.5%), but this is outweighed by its much higher expense ratio, lower dividend yield, and narrower focus on large- and mid-cap stocks only.
Mentioned as a top holding in both ETFs; no specific sentiment is expressed about the company itself, only its presence as a major position in both portfolios.
Mentioned as a top holding in both ETFs; no specific sentiment is expressed about the company itself, only its presence as a major position in both portfolios.

title: Emerging Markets Are Crushing the S&P 500: Is the Rally Just Beginning?
author: Mike Zaccardi, Cfa, Cmt
description: Emerging markets are significantly outperforming the S&P 500, with IEMG returning 44% over the past 12 months compared to SPY's lower returns. The rally is driven by broad country leadership and strong relative strength rather than just dollar weakness. Technical analysis suggests further upside potential, with price targets around $98 for IEMG, though near-term calendar risks and potential headwinds from rising interest rates and currency movements warrant caution.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: IEMG has returned 44% in the past 12 months, tripled SPY's performance year-over-year, and recently broke through early-2021 peaks. Technical analysis suggests further upside with price targets around $98, and the rally appears to be in early stages with strong relative strength.

SPY has significantly underperformed emerging markets, with IEMG returning approximately three times its performance over the past year. The article positions U.S. equities as lagging in the current market environment.

EEM has gained 10% year-to-date and is part of the emerging markets rally. The article notes it has matched its best historical performance out of annual starting gates, though it is less cost-efficient than IEMG.
VWO is mentioned as a viable emerging markets ETF option alongside IEMG and EEM, benefiting from the broad emerging markets rally.
South Korea ETF is highlighted as one of the leading emerging market performers, continuing to outperform after essentially zero returns from 2007-2023.
Brazil ETF is noted as a popular emerging market play and one of the leading performers, though it carries commodity price sensitivity risk.

title: How Does IEMG's Growth Focus Against IXUS' Broader International Diversification?
author: Adé Hennis
description: IEMG and IXUS offer different approaches to international stock exposure. IEMG focuses on emerging markets with higher tech concentration, while IXUS provides broader diversification across developed and emerging markets. Despite IEMG's stronger 1-year performance (37.83% vs 31.67%), IXUS has delivered superior 5-year returns and lower volatility, making it the more attractive option for most investors seeking international exposure.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: IEMG shows strong 1-year performance (37.83%) and higher dividend yield (2.51%), but underperforms IXUS over 5 years with higher volatility (37.16% max drawdown). It remains a viable option for growth-focused investors seeking concentrated tech exposure, but lacks the diversification advantage of IXUS.

IXUS demonstrates superior long-term performance with 35% higher price returns since inception and outperformance of 20% over the past 5 years. It offers broader diversification across developed and emerging markets with lower volatility, making it the recommended choice for most international equity investors.
Mentioned as a top holding in both IEMG and IXUS, indicating significant exposure to this company in both funds. No specific performance commentary provided.

title: How Does IEMG's Emerging Markets Potential Compare to SPGM's Global Exposure?
author: Adé Hennis
description: IEMG and SPGM are two ETFs offering different investment approaches: IEMG focuses exclusively on emerging markets with higher recent returns (37.83% in 1-year) but greater volatility, while SPGM provides broader global exposure including U.S. stocks with more consistent long-term growth. Both have identical expense ratios of 0.09%, but investors should choose based on their risk tolerance and investment timeline.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: IEMG demonstrates strong recent performance with 37.83% 1-year returns and higher dividend yield (2.51%), making it attractive for growth-focused investors seeking emerging market exposure. However, the article notes higher volatility and risk associated with younger companies in emerging markets.
Mentioned as a top holding in IEMG, representing the fund's exposure to Asian tech giants. No specific sentiment expressed about the company itself.
